Party_Details.Name: Hau Tau Thong

Facts.Text: The individual served as a managing director and equal shareholder of the family-owned restaurant group.
Following a forensic report identifying $153,000 in cash discrepancies, the director sought to exercise his statutory right to inspect the company's accounting records.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director can enforce the statutory right of inspection when corporate records are held exclusively by a hostile co-director during a management impasse.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 199(3) of the Companies Act, a director’s right of inspection is an absolute incident of the office and can only be displaced by clear proof of an improper purpose causing substantial harm to the entity.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff sought inspection to verify financial data. The Defendants resisted, alleging he sought 'ammunition' for personal litigation, which they characterized as an improper private purpose.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found the right of inspection is a fundamental power required for a director to discharge management obligations. As no evidence of intent to injure the company was provided, the statutory right remained enforceable.

Party_Details.Name: Hau Tau Khang

Facts.Text: The individual took possession of the keys to the companies' documentation cabinets and refused to grant access to his co-director.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director can unilaterally block a co-director's access to corporate property based on an ongoing shareholder dispute.
Party_Details.Rule: A director has no standing to withhold corporate accounts from an equal board member; such obstruction interferes with the collective management obligations of the board.
Party_Details.Application: The Defendant asserted that providing access would bypass discovery rules in the related derivative action. The Plaintiff asserted the records were company property he was entitled to review.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court ordered the Defendant to provide access. It held that informational parity is essential for corporate governance and the existence of litigation does not suspend a director's statutory rights.

Party_Details.Name: Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd & Sanur Holding Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The entities were a group of companies operating Indonesian restaurants that had ceased operations following a breakdown in director relationships.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company is obligated to produce its accounts for a director accused of operational and financial mismanagement.
Party_Details.Rule: A company’s duty to allow inspection under section 199(3) is a mandatory statutory obligation that protects the integrity of corporate management.
Party_Details.Application: The companies resisted the demand for inspection on the basis that the requesting director was in a position of conflict.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The entities were found liable to facilitate the inspection. The court determined that clarifying a company's financial status is a legitimate directorial function regardless of the internal dispute.

Party_Details.Name: Hau Tau Thong

Facts.Text: The individual applied for specific discovery of company accounts while a separate application for leave to commence a derivative action was still pending.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a party is entitled to specific discovery at the preliminary 'leave' stage of a derivative action (Section 216A) before a prima facie case has been established.
Party_Details.Rule: Discovery is generally unavailable at the leave stage unless the documents are absolutely necessary to resolve the threshold inquiry of whether the action is in the company's interests.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff (by application) sought early discovery to save costs and clarify the merits. The Defendants argued that granting discovery at this stage would allow a bypass of procedural safeguards.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court dismissed the application. It held that the leave stage is a low-threshold filter intended to stop meritless claims; granting full discovery at this stage is premature and an abuse of process.

Party_Details.Name: Hau Tau Khang

Facts.Text: The director initiated a leave application to commence a derivative action for alleged breaches of management obligations by his brother.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director should be forced to provide discovery in a summary proceeding where the merits of the underlying action have not yet been joined.
Party_Details.Rule: Specific discovery follows the filing of pleadings; it is not a tool to be utilized during the initial filtration of a derivative action claim.
Party_Details.Application: The Defendant successfully resisted the discovery application, arguing it was an attempt to litigate the merits before a court had authorized the company to sue.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court ruled in favor of the Defendant. It determined that discovery of accounts would not dispose of the non-financial allegations in the leave stage and was not necessary for the threshold inquiry.

Party_Details.Name: Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd & Sanur Holding Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The entities were the proposed beneficiaries of a lawsuit intended to recover funds allegedly diverted to a competing business venture.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company should be required to facilitate discovery during the preliminary stage of a derivative action ostensibly brought for its benefit.
Party_Details.Rule: A company is a nominal party in a derivative action leave stage; discovery orders against it are subject to a strict necessity test.
Party_Details.Application: The companies were involved in a dispute over whether a derivative action against one of their own directors was commercially justified at that stage.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that discovery at this preliminary stage would not assist in the summary threshold inquiry required to grant leave for a derivative action.
